Paul confronts the Corinthian church with a sin so grave that even pagan society recognizes its depravity—a man living with his father's wife—yet the church has responded with pride rather than grief. What's stunning here is not merely the sin itself, but the church's moral blindness to it. They have become so comfortable in their spiritual environment that they've lost the capacity to be grieved by wickedness. Paul's response is not gentle correction; it is apostolic judgment. He pronounces that this man be handed over to Satan for the destruction of his flesh, that his spirit may be saved. This language shocks modern ears, but Paul isn't being cruel. He's recognizing that sometimes the most loving thing the church can do is enforce real consequences for unrepentant sin—to let a person experience the full weight of their rebellion so that, confronted with its reality, they might turn back to Christ. The goal is redemption, but the path to it runs through accountability.

The image of leaven spreading through the whole batch of dough reveals Paul's deeper concern. One person's unrepentant sin doesn't stay isolated; it corrupts the community's witness and standard. The church has become proud of its tolerance, its acceptance, its refusal to judge. But this isn't virtue—it's complicity. Paul reminds them who they are: they are the unleavened batch because Christ, their Passover Lamb, has been sacrificed. That foundational reality should shape everything about how they live together. They are a redeemed people, separated unto holiness, living in light of Christ's work. The old leaven of malice and wickedness has no place in a community that claims to belong to the crucified and risen Lord.

Yet Paul is careful here. When he commands them not to associate with sexually immoral people, he clarifies—he doesn't mean the immoral of the world. That would require leaving the world entirely, which is neither possible nor the church's calling. The church's responsibility is to judge and discipline those within its own fellowship who claim the name of Christ while living in open sin. This is an exercise of love, not arrogance. God Himself will judge those outside. But inside the church, holiness matters. Standards matter. Not because the church is better than the world, but because the church has been bought with a price and called to a different way. When a brother refuses to turn from sexual immorality, greed, abuse, or idolatry, the church's silence becomes a lie about what Christ has done and who He is.

The weight of this chapter is this: grace and holiness are not opposed. Real love sometimes means saying no, drawing boundaries, and letting someone experience the consequences of their rebellion. A church that will not do this has abandoned both love and truth. It has forgotten that it exists not for the comfort of its members, but for the glory of Christ and the redemption of souls. The question Paul leaves with you is uncomfortable: When you know of unrepentant sin in your church, do you grieve? Or do you rationalize, excuse, and protect the status quo? Your answer reveals whether you truly believe that Christ has made you clean.
